message.content:
I haven't been working on this project for months. I remember we were stuck on tests and I felt completely overwhelmed by my efforts to get tests to be well structured, complete, efficient and useful. Way to much effort has gone into that. The project scope is not that big, but after weeks of work, we are still stuck on the framework (which should be more or less ok now) and the tests for it.

We followed github speckit as dev workflow.
Haven't used this anymore since: switched to more lightweight superpowers and now GSD.
So I'd rather continue the GSD way, but keep the ADR, I think that is a good thing.

Most of all, I want to take a step back and see
- what is the status?
- how do we get as fast as possible to first useful functionality (sync home dir and sync packages), at good quality

So I want you to take a critical look at where we are and how this project is organized and propose a way forward that is more efficient than the weeks we spent without delivering any useful functionality.
So give a concise status report and proposal. Of course, spar with me where you deem that useful.
Don't ask me about the status; I don't remember; look at codebase.

- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- code-simplifier:code-simplifier: Simplifies and refines code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. Focuses on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- gsd-advisor-researcher: Researches a single gray area decision and returns a structured comparison table with rationale. Spawned by discuss-phase advisor mode. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*)

- gsd-ai-researcher: Researches a chosen AI framework's official docs to produce implementation-ready guidance — best practices, syntax, core patterns, and pitfalls distilled for the specific use case. Writes the Framework Quick Reference and Implementation Guidance sections of AI-SPEC.md. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch, mcp__context7__*)
- gsd-assumptions-analyzer: Deeply analyzes codebase for a phase and returns structured assumptions with evidence. Spawned by discuss-phase assumptions mode. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-code-fixer: Applies fixes to code review findings from REVIEW.md. Reads source files, applies intelligent fixes, and commits each fix atomically. Spawned by /gsd-code-review --fix. (Tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-code-reviewer: Reviews source files for bugs, security issues, and code quality problems. Produces structured REVIEW.md with severity-classified findings. Spawned by /gsd-code-review. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-codebase-mapper: Explores codebase and writes structured analysis documents. Spawned by map-codebase with a focus area (tech, arch, quality, concerns). Writes documents directly to reduce orchestrator context load. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Write, Skill)

- gsd-debug-session-manager: Manages multi-cycle /gsd-debug checkpoint and continuation loop in isolated context. Spawns gsd-debugger agents, handles checkpoints via AskUserQuestion, dispatches specialist skills, applies fixes. Returns compact summary to main context. Spawned by /gsd-debug command. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Agent, AskUserQuestion)

- gsd-debugger: Investigates bugs using scientific method, manages debug sessions, handles checkpoints. Spawned by /gsd-debug orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch)

- gsd-doc-classifier: Classifies a single planning document as ADR, PRD, SPEC, DOC, or UNKNOWN. Extracts title, scope summary, and cross-references. Spawned in parallel by /gsd-ingest-docs. Writes a JSON classification file and returns a one-line confirmation. (Tools: Read, Write, Grep, Glob)

- gsd-doc-synthesizer: Synthesizes classified planning docs into a single consolidated context. Applies precedence rules, detects cross-ref cycles, enforces LOCKED-vs-LOCKED hard-blocks, and writes INGEST-CONFLICTS.md with three buckets (auto-resolved, competing-variants, unresolved-blockers). Spawned by /gsd-ingest-docs. (Tools: Read, Write, Grep, Glob, Bash)
- gsd-doc-verifier: Verifies factual claims in generated docs against the live codebase. Returns structured JSON per doc. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob)

- gsd-doc-writer: Writes and updates project documentation. Spawned with a doc_assignment block specifying doc type, mode (create/update/supplement), and project context. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit, Skill)

- gsd-domain-researcher: Researches the business domain and real-world application context of the AI system being built. Surfaces domain expert evaluation criteria, industry-specific failure modes, regulatory context, and what "good" looks like for practitioners in this field — before the eval-planner turns it into measurable rubrics. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*)

- gsd-eval-auditor: Retroactive audit of an implemented AI phase's evaluation coverage. Checks implementation against the AI-SPEC.md evaluation plan. Scores each eval dimension as COVERED/PARTIAL/MISSING. Produces a scored EVAL-REVIEW.md with findings, gaps, and remediation guidance. Spawned by /gsd-eval-review orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-eval-planner: Designs a structured evaluation strategy for an AI phase. Identifies critical failure modes, selects eval dimensions with rubrics, recommends tooling, and specifies the reference dataset. Writes the Evaluation Strategy, Guardrails, and Production Monitoring sections of AI-SPEC.md. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, AskUserQuestion)

- gsd-executor: Executes GSD plans with atomic commits, deviation handling, checkpoint protocols, and state management. Spawned by execute-phase orchestrator or execute-plan command. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, mcp__context7__*)

- gsd-framework-selector: Presents an interactive decision matrix to surface the right AI/LLM framework for the user's specific use case. Produces a scored recommendation with rationale. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase and /gsd-select-framework orchestrators. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebSearch, AskUserQuestion)
- gsd-integration-checker: Verifies cross-phase integration and E2E flows. Checks that phases connect properly and user workflows complete end-to-end. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)
- gsd-intel-updater: Analyzes codebase and writes structured intel files to .planning/intel/. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- gsd-mempalace-curator: Ship-time MemPalace curation — writes the session diary, proposes/creates cross-project tunnels, mirrors extract-learnings into the temporal KG, and runs wing-scoped drawer pruning. Spawned at ship:post by the mempalace capability. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob)
- gsd-nyquist-auditor: Fills Nyquist validation gaps by generating tests and verifying coverage for phase requirements (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-pattern-mapper: Analyzes codebase for existing patterns and produces PATTERNS.md mapping new files to closest analogs. Read-only codebase analysis spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator before planning. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Write)

- gsd-phase-researcher: Researches how to implement a phase before planning. Produces RESEARCH.md consumed by gsd-planner. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__firecrawl__*, mcp__exa__*, mcp__tavily__*, mcp__ref__*, mcp__jina__*, mcp__perplexity__*)
- gsd-plan-checker: Verifies plans will achieve phase goal before execution. Goal-backward analysis of plan quality. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-planner: Creates executable phase plans with task breakdown, dependency analysis, and goal-backward verification. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*)

- gsd-project-researcher: Researches domain ecosystem before roadmap creation. Produces files in .planning/research/ consumed during roadmap creation. Spawned by /gsd-new-project or /gsd-new-milestone orchestrators. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__firecrawl__*, mcp__exa__*, mcp__tavily__*, mcp__ref__*, mcp__jina__*, mcp__perplexity__*)

- gsd-research-synthesizer: Synthesizes research outputs from parallel researcher agents into SUMMARY.md. Spawned by /gsd-new-project after 4 researcher agents complete. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Skill)

- gsd-roadmapper: Creates project roadmaps with phase breakdown, requirement mapping, success criteria derivation, and coverage validation. Spawned by /gsd-new-project orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-security-auditor: Verifies threat mitigations from PLAN.md threat model exist in implemented code. Produces SECURITY.md. Spawned by /gsd-secure-phase. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)
- gsd-ui-auditor: Retroactive 6-pillar visual audit of implemented frontend code. Produces scored UI-REVIEW.md. Spawned by /gsd-ui-review orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)
- gsd-ui-checker: Validates UI-SPEC.md design contracts against 6 quality dimensions. Produces BLOCK/FLAG/PASS verdicts. Spawned by /gsd-ui-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-ui-researcher: Produces UI-SPEC.md design contract for frontend phases. Reads upstream artifacts, detects design system state, asks only unanswered questions. Spawned by /gsd-ui-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__firecrawl__*, mcp__exa__*, mcp__tavily__*, mcp__ref__*, mcp__jina__*)

- gsd-user-profiler: Analyzes extracted session messages across 8 behavioral dimensions to produce a scored developer profile with confidence levels and evidence. Spawned by profile orchestration workflows. (Tools: Read)

- gsd-verifier: Verifies phase goal achievement through goal-backward analysis. Checks codebase delivers what phase promised, not just that tasks completed. Creates VERIFICATION.md report. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to review code for adherence to project guidelines, style guides, and best practices. This agent should be used proactively after writing or modifying code, especially before committing changes or creating pull requests. It will check for style violations, potential issues, and ensure code follows the established patterns in CLAUDE.md. Also the agent needs to know which files to focus on for the review. In most cases this will be recently completed work which is unstaged in git (can be retrieved by running git diff). However there can be cases where this is different, make sure to specify this as the agent input when calling the agent. Typical triggers include the user asking for a review of a feature they just implemented, the assistant proactively reviewing its own newly-written code before declaring a task done, and a final pre-PR check before opening a pull request. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-simplifier: Use this agent when code has been written or modified and needs to be simplified for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. This agent should be triggered automatically after completing a coding task or writing a logical chunk of code. It simplifies code by following project best practices while retaining all functionality. The agent focuses only on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise.

Examples:

<example>
Context: The assistant has just implemented a new feature that adds user authentication to an API endpoint.
user: "Please add authentication to the /api/users endpoint"
assistant: "I've implemented the authentication for the /api/users endpoint. Here's the code:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
Since a logical chunk of code has been written (authentication feature), use the Task tool to launch the code-simplifier agent to improve the code's clarity and maintainability while preserving functionality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now let me use the code-simplifier agent to refine this implementation for better clarity and maintainability"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just fixed a bug by adding several conditional checks.
user: "Fix the null pointer exception in the data processor"
assistant: "I've added the necessary null checks to prevent the exception:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After modifying code to fix a bug, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the fix follows best practices and maintains code quality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Let me refine this bug fix using the code-simplifier agent to ensure it follows our best practices"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just refactored a function to improve performance.
user: "Optimize the data sorting algorithm for better performance"
assistant: "I've optimized the sorting algorithm. Here's the updated implementation:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After completing a performance optimization task, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and maintainable.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now I'll use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and follows our coding standards"
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:comment-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to analyze code comments for accuracy, completeness, and long-term maintainability. This includes (1) after generating large documentation comments or docstrings, (2) before finalizing a pull request that adds or modifies comments, (3) when reviewing existing comments for potential technical debt or comment rot, and (4) when you need to verify that comments accurately reflect the code they describe. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:pr-test-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to review a pull request for test coverage quality and completeness. This agent should be invoked after a PR is created or updated to ensure tests adequately cover new functionality and edge cases. Typical triggers include the user asking whether tests on a freshly-created PR are thorough, an updated PR adding new logic that needs coverage analysis, and a final pre-merge double-check before marking a PR ready. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:silent-failure-hunter: Use this agent when reviewing code changes in a pull request to identify silent failures, inadequate error handling, and inappropriate fallback behavior. This agent should be invoked proactively after completing a logical chunk of work that involves error handling, catch blocks, fallback logic, or any code that could potentially suppress errors. Examples:

<example>
Context: Daisy has just finished implementing a new feature that fetches data from an API with fallback behavior.
Daisy: "I've added error handling to the API client. Can you review it?"
Assistant: "Let me use the silent-failure-hunter agent to thoroughly examine the error handling in your changes."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has created a PR with changes that include try-catch blocks.
Daisy: "Please review PR #1234"
Assistant: "I'll use the silent-failure-hunter agent to check for any silent failures or inadequate error handling in this PR."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has just refactored error handling code.
Daisy: "I've updated the error handling in the authentication module"
Assistant: "Let me proactively use the silent-failure-hunter agent to ensure the error handling changes don't introduce silent failures."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:type-design-analyzer: Use this agent when you need expert analysis of type design in your codebase. Specifically use it (1) when introducing a new type to ensure it follows best practices for encapsulation and invariant expression, (2) during pull request creation to review all types being added, and (3) when refactoring existing types to improve their design quality. The agent will provide both qualitative feedback and quantitative ratings on encapsulation, invariant expression, usefulness, and enforcement. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

- do-issue: Autonomous issue-to-PR workflow using GSD. Argument: GitHub issue number.
- merge-main: Merge the base branch into the current branch, resolving conflicts locally (including smart 3-way...
- review-code-structure: Review the python code of this project with a focus on code architecture and code design. Elimina...
- speckit.analyze: Perform a non-destructive cross-artifact consistency and quality analysis across spec.md, plan.md, and tasks.md after task generation.
- speckit.checklist: Generate a custom checklist for the current feature based on user requirements.
- speckit.clarify: Identify underspecified areas in the current feature spec by asking up to 5 highly targeted clarification questions and encoding answers back into the spec.
- speckit.consolidate: Consolidate SpecKit artifacts into the Golden Copy living docs in docs/system/ per ADR-011
- speckit.constitution: Create or update the project constitution from interactive or provided principle inputs, ensuring all dependent templates stay in sync.
- speckit.implement: Execute the implementation plan by processing and executing all tasks defined in tasks.md
- speckit.implement.resolve-feedback: Resolve feedback received on a speckit feature implementation
- speckit.implement.resolve-follow-up: Resolve feedback received on a speckit feature implementation
- speckit.implement.review: Review a speckit feature implementation
- speckit.plan: Execute the implementation planning workflow using the plan template to generate design artifacts.
- speckit.plan.resolve-feedback: Resolve feedback on a speckit feature plan
- speckit.plan.resolve-follow-up: Resolve feedback received on a speckit feature implementation
- speckit.plan.review-architecture: Review a speckit feature plan
- speckit.plan.review-detail: Review a speckit feature plan
- speckit.specify: Create or update the feature specification from a natural language feature description.
- speckit.specify.resolve-feedback: Resolve feedback received on a speckit feature plan
- speckit.specify.review: Review a speckit feature specification
- speckit.tasks: Generate an actionable, dependency-ordered tasks.md for the feature based on available design artifacts.
- speckit.tasks.resolve-feedback: Resolve feedback received on a speckit feature tasks list
- speckit.taskstoissues: Convert existing tasks into actionable, dependency-ordered GitHub issues for the feature based on available design artifacts.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- pr-review-toolkit:review-pr: Comprehensive PR review using specialized agents
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

lastPrompt: I haven't been working on this project for months. I remember we were stuck on tests and I felt completely overwhelmed by my efforts to get tests to be well structured, complete, efficient and useful.…

aiTitle: Assess project status and streamline path to first useful features

content.text: I'll dig into the codebase to get a real picture before proposing anything. Let me fan out a few parallel explorations and check some basics directly.

input.prompt:
Explore the source code in src/pcswitcher/ of this project (a Linux PC-state sync tool). I need a precise inventory of what is actually IMPLEMENTED and working vs. stubbed/placeholder.

For each module in src/pcswitcher/ (including jobs/), give me 1-2 lines: what it does, and whether it's real working code or a stub/dummy/placeholder.

Pay special attention to:
- The orchestrator and job framework: how do jobs get registered and run? Is it a real working pipeline?

- The CLI: what commands actually work end to end?
- config_sync.py and sync_history.py: what do they do?

Be concrete. End with a short bullet list: "Actually works end-to-end:" vs "Framework/scaffolding only:" vs "Not started:". Reference file:line where useful. Do not read test files.

Read these planning docs in this repo and summarize concisely:
- docs/planning/High level requirements.md
- docs/planning/Feature breakdown.md
- docs/adr/_index.md (and skim the ADR titles)

I need to understand: what is the actual MVP / first useful functionality for this "PC-switcher" tool (sync between Linux desktops)? The user wants to get to "sync home dir" and "sync packages" as fast as possible.

Tell me:
1. What does the High level requirements doc say is the core workflow and MVP scope? Quote the key parts.

5. Is there a defined "job" abstraction these sync operations are meant to plug into?

Be concise but quote specifics. Reference file paths/sections.

file.content:
# PC-switcher configuration file
# Location: ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
# Documentation: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher

# =============================================================================
# Logging Configuration (3-setting model)
# =============================================================================
# Controls where log messages appear based on their severity level.
#
# Log levels (from most to least verbose):
#   DEBUG (10)    - Internal diagnostics, very verbose
#   FULL (15)     - Operational details (file-level sync info)
#   INFO (20)     - High-level progress and status updates
#   WARNING (30)  - Unexpected but non-fatal conditions
#   ERROR (40)    - Recoverable errors
#   CRITICAL (50) - Unrecoverable errors, sync must abort

logging:
  # file: Floor level for log FILE output (~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/)
  #       Messages at or above this level are written to the log file.
  file: DEBUG

  # tui: Floor level for TERMINAL output during sync
  #      Messages at or above this level are shown in the terminal UI.
  tui: INFO

  # external: Additional floor for THIRD-PARTY libraries (asyncssh, etc.)
  #           External library logs must pass BOTH this floor AND the
  #           destination floor (file or tui) to appear in output.
  #           Use DEBUG to see SSH connection details, WARNING to suppress noise.
  external: WARNING

# =============================================================================
# Sync Jobs Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Enable or disable optional sync jobs
# Note: Only jobs explicitly listed here will be discovered and run
# Set to false to disable a job (e.g., dummy_fail: false)

sync_jobs:
  # Test jobs (for development/testing)
  dummy_success: true    # Test job that completes successfully
  dummy_fail: false      # Test job that fails at configurable progress

  # Future jobs (uncomment when available):
  # user_data: true       # Sync /home and /root directories
  # packages: true        # Sync apt, snap, flatpak packages
  # docker: false         # Sync Docker images, containers, volumes
  # vms: false            # Sync KVM/virt-manager VMs
  # k3s: false            # Sync k3s cluster state

# =============================================================================
# Disk Space Monitoring
# =============================================================================
# Configuration for disk space checks before and during sync
# Threshold format: percentage (e.g., "20%") or absolute (e.g., "50GiB")

disk_space_monitor:
  # Minimum free space required before sync starts
  preflight_minimum: "20%"

  # Minimum free space required during sync (CRITICAL - abort if below)
  runtime_minimum: "15%"

  # Free space threshold for warnings during sync
  warning_threshold: "25%"

  # Seconds between disk space checks during sync
  check_interval: 30

# =============================================================================
# Btrfs Snapshot Management
# =============================================================================
# Configuration for automatic btrfs snapshots before and after sync
# IMPORTANT: Subvolumes must match YOUR system's btrfs layout

btrfs_snapshots:
  # List of btrfs subvolumes to snapshot
  # Check your system: sudo btrfs subvolume list /
  # Common layouts:
  #   - Ubuntu default: ["@", "@home"]
  #   - Single root: ["@"]
  subvolumes:
    - "@"
    - "@home"

  # Number of recent sync sessions to always retain snapshots for,
  # irrespective of age (max_age_days below)
  keep_recent: 3

  # Maximum age in days for snapshot retention (optional)
  # Note that always keeping 'keep_recent' snapshots takes precedence.
  # Uncomment to enable age-based cleanup:
  # max_age_days: 7

# =============================================================================
# Job-Specific Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Each sync job can have its own configuration section
# Configuration keys must match the job name

# Test job configurations (examples)
dummy_success:
  source_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on target machine

dummy_fail:
  source_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on target machine
  fail_at: 12            # Elapsed seconds at which to fail

# Future job configurations will be added here when features are implemented:

content.text: The picture is crisp. The entire **plumbing** is built and green (locks, SSH, snapshots, install-on-target, config-sync, disk monitor, UI, logging, 10-phase orchestrator) — but the only sync "jobs" that exist are `dummy_success`/`dummy_fail`. `user_data` (home) and `packages` are commented-out "future jobs." Let me see what implementing a real job actually takes, and where work was left.

<result>Based on my exploration of the source code in `src/pcswitcher/`, here's a precise inventory:

## MODULE INVENTORY

**Core Infrastructure (All Working):**
- **orchestrator.py** - Real working orchestration: manages 10-phase sync pipeline (lock acquisition → SSH connection → validation → snapshots → job execution → history update) with proper async task management
- **connection.py** - Real SSH connection management using asyncssh with multiplexing and keepalive
- **executor.py** - Real command execution framework with LocalExecutor and RemoteExecutor, process streaming
- **config_sync.py** - Real implementation: fetches target config, shows diffs, prompts user choices, copies file via SFTP (lines 30-317)
- **sync_history.py** - Real implementation: tracks SOURCE/TARGET role in JSON file, detects consecutive syncs (lines 36-149)
- **config.py** - Real config loading with YAML parsing and JSON schema validation
- **cli.py** - Real CLI with 5 commands: `sync`, `logs`, `cleanup-snapshots`, `init`, `self update` (line 182+)

**Job Framework (Real):**
- **jobs/base.py** - Working abstract base classes: `Job`, `SystemJob`, `SyncJob`, `BackgroundJob` with validation/execution pipeline
- **jobs/context.py** - JobContext with executors, event bus, config
- **jobs/__init__.py** - Job registration/discovery (lines 1-24)

**System Jobs (Real, Working):**
- **jobs/install_on_target.py** (lines 17-134) - Real implementation: version checking, installs/upgrades pc-switcher on target, verifies installation
- **jobs/btrfs.py** (lines 48-211) - Real implementation: creates pre/post sync btrfs snapshots on both source and target with session folder organization
- **jobs/disk_space_monitor.py** (lines 27-239) - Real background job: continuous disk monitoring with configurable thresholds (percent or absolute), raises `DiskSpaceCriticalError` if critical

**Test/Dummy Jobs (Stub for Infrastructure Testing):**
- **jobs/dummy_success.py** - Dummy job: simulates work with configurable duration on source/target, logs at intervals, reports progress (0→25→50→75→100%), executes real bash on target
- **jobs/dummy_fail.py** - Dummy job: fails at configurable elapsed time, for testing error handling

**Infrastructure (Real):**
- **models.py** - Type definitions (Job, Host, SessionStatus, ValidationError, ConfigError, etc.)
- **events.py** - EventBus and event types
- **logger.py** - Logging setup with TUI + file output
- **ui.py** - Terminal UI with progress bar
- **disk.py** - Disk space checking functions
- **btrfs_snapshots.py** - Btrfs snapshot operations
- **lock.py** - Lock management for concurrent sync prevention
- **version.py** - Version/release parsing
- **install.py** - Install script URL generation

---

## KEY FINDINGS

**Job Framework:** Real, working pipeline. Orchestrator dynamically discovers enabled jobs by name (lines 521-530, orchestrator.py), validates config via JSON schema, validates system state, executes sequentially, and tracks results.

**Concrete Sync Jobs that Actually Work:**
1. ✅ **BtrfsSnapshotJob** - Creates actual snapshots pre/post sync (both hosts)
2. ✅ **InstallOnTargetJob** - Installs/upgrades pc-switcher on target
3. ✅ **DiskSpaceMonitorJob** - Real background monitoring during sync
4. ✅ **ConfigSync** - Reads config, fetches remote, shows diffs, copies file

**Jobs That Don't Exist (Mentioned in config as "Future"):**
- ❌ **user_data** - Would sync `/home` and `/root` - NOT IMPLEMENTED
- ❌ **packages** - Would sync apt/snap/flatpak packages - NOT IMPLEMENTED
- ❌ **docker, vms, k3s** - Listed in config comments only - NOT IMPLEMENTED

**CLI End-to-End:**
- ✅ `pc-switcher sync <target>` - Full pipeline works (lock → SSH → validate → snapshots → install → config sync → execute jobs → history)
- ✅ `pc-switcher init` - Creates default config
- ✅ `pc-switcher logs` - Views log files with formatting
- ✅ `pc-switcher cleanup-snapshots` - Age/count-based cleanup
- ✅ `pc-switcher self update` - Version management

---

## SUMMARY

**Actually works end-to-end:**
- Orchestrator and job framework (dynamic discovery, validation, execution pipeline) — orchestrator.py:521-579
- Config sync with user prompts and SFTP copy — config_sync.py:237-317
- Sync history tracking and consecutive-sync warnings — sync_history.py:36-149
- Btrfs snapshots (pre/post) — jobs/btrfs.py:122-210
- PC-switcher installation/upgrade on target — jobs/install_on_target.py:67-134
- Disk space monitoring with thresholds — jobs/disk_space_monitor.py:126-221
- CLI: sync, logs, init, cleanup-snapshots, self update commands — cli.py:182-592
- SSH connection, lock management, async task coordination

**Framework/scaffolding only (no real sync):**
- Test jobs (dummy_success, dummy_fail) for infrastructure validation
- Job discovery system ready for user_data, packages, docker, vms, k3s (config comments show intent but no implementations)

**Not started:**
- Any actual file/home directory sync job
- Any package manager integration
- Docker, VM, or k3s state sync

The core PC-switcher **orchestration, configuration sync, snapshot safety, and installation automation are fully implemented**. The missing piece is the actual **data sync jobs** (files, packages, etc.) — the framework is ready for them but they don't exist yet.</result>

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Based on my review of the planning docs and system specifications, here's what I found:

## 1. MVP Scope: Core Workflow & Scope

**High Level Requirements (6510 bytes, "## Workflow" section):**

> "Simple workflow: 1) Work on source machine, 2) Startup/awaken target machine if not started yet, 3) Manual trigger sync from source to target, 4) Wait for sync to complete, 5) Resume work"

From **Feature Breakdown**, the MVP consists of:
- **Items 1-4 (Core/Infrastructure — P1 priorities)**:
  1. Basic CLI & Infrastructure
  2. Safety Infrastructure (btrfs snapshot management)
  3. Installation & Setup
  4. Rollback Capability
  
- **Items 5-6 (User Features — the actual sync jobs)**:
  5. **User Data Sync** — `/home` and `/root` with metadata, selective caches, exclusions, conflict detection
  6. **Package Management Sync** — apt/snap/flatpak/PPAs/manual installs, detect conflicts/version mismatches

**Key user story constraint**: "Only one machine is actively used at a time" — no bi-directional sync in MVP.

---

## 2. Sync Home Directory: Technical Design

**Requirements (High Level Requirements, "What Must Sync"):**

> "User data (`/home`, `/root`)
> - All documents, code, configs, application data
> - Selective caches: dev tool caches (`uv`, `pip`, `cargo`, `npm`) synced; browser/IDE caches excluded
> - Specific mention for VS Code"

**Critical constraint (Environment section):**

> "All machines have a single btrfs filesystem with a flat layout (all synced data on one filesystem)"

**Technical approach NOT yet specified.** The detailed job spec for User Data Sync doesn't exist in the docs yet. The architecture allows for task-specific implementations (bash scripts via Python orchestration per ADR-003), but no decision on rsync vs. btrfs snapshots vs. custom sync method is documented.

---

## 3. Sync Packages: Technical Design

**Requirements (High Level Requirements):**

> "Installed packages: apt, snap, flatpak, manual .debs, custom PPAs, packages installed through install scripts"
> 
> "Each includes sync logic, conflict detection, and validation."

**Technical approach: NOT yet detailed.** No job implementation or spec exists. The Feature Breakdown says packages must "detect package conflicts or version mismatches," but the actual mechanism (list comparison, dpkg/rpm query, etc.) is unspecified.

---

## 4. Locked-In Architectural Constraints

**ADR-002 (SSH Communication Channel)**:
- Single persistent SSH connection with multiplexing (`ControlMaster`), not per-operation
- Target-side scripts produce **line-buffered stdout** for progress (no polling)
- Exit codes for success/failure; stdout/stderr separated
- "Forbidden": custom protocols, daemons on target, per-operation SSH connections

**ADR-003 (Python + Task-Specific Languages)**:
- Python orchestrator (Python 3.14, `uv` for dependency management, `asyncssh` library)
- Task-specific bash scripts for individual sync operations (bundled in `scripts/`)
- All orchestration logic in Python; delegation to bash where appropriate

**ADR-005 (Asyncio for Concurrency)**:
- **All Job methods MUST be `async def`** (`validate()`, `execute()`)
- Use `asyncssh` for remote operations (cancellable)
- Use `asyncio.TaskGroup` for structured concurrency
- Signal handlers trigger `asyncio.Task.cancel()` for immediate abort

**ADR-010 (Logging Infrastructure)**:
- stdlib `logging` module with custom Handler
- File logging (JSON), TUI logging (Rich-formatted)
- Configurable log levels separately for file and TUI

---

## 5. Job Abstraction

**From `/src/pcswitcher/jobs/base.py`:**

```python
class Job(ABC):
    name: ClassVar[str]
    required: ClassVar[bool]
    CONFIG_SCHEMA: ClassVar[dict] = {}
    
    # Async interface
    async def validate(self) -> list[ValidationError]: ...
    async def execute(self) -> None: ...
    
    # Helpers provided to jobs
    @property
    def source(self) -> LocalExecutor: ...  # For local operations
    @property
    def target(self) -> RemoteExecutor: ...  # For remote SSH operations
    
    def _log(host: Host, level: LogLevel, message: str, **extra) -> None
    def _report_progress(update: ProgressUpdate) -> None
    def _validation_error(host: Host, message: str) -> ValidationError
```

**Subclasses:**
- `SystemJob(Job)` — infrastructure (btrfs snapshots, installation); `required=True`, not in config
- `SyncJob(Job)` — user-facing features; `required=False`, configurable via `sync_jobs` in config.yaml
- `BackgroundJob(Job)` — parallel execution (disk monitoring); runs throughout sync

**Every sync job gets**:
- Configuration validation via JSON Schema (declared in `CONFIG_SCHEMA`)
- Logging at 6 levels (DEBUG, FULL, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL)
- Progress reporting with percent/current/total/item
- SSH executors for both source and target
- Graceful cancellation via `asyncio.CancelledError`

---

## Summary for MVP Sprint

To reach "sync home dir + sync packages fast":

1. ✅ **Infrastructure (already implemented)**: Core orchestrator, SSH layer, asyncio concurrency, btrfs snapshot jobs, logging
2. ❌ **Blocking issues (3 of them per Issue Triage 2025-12-31)**:
   - #103: Integrate stdlib logging (diagnostics for third-party libs)
   - #37: Add `--dry-run` flag (safety before sync)

<task-id>a8f3a4a6511513d91</task-id>
<tool-use-id>REDACTED</tool-use-id>

<summary>Agent "Summarize requirements and MVP scope" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Based on my review of the planning docs and system specifications, here's what I found:

## 1. MVP Scope: Core Workflow & Scope

**High Level Requirements (6510 bytes, "## Workflow" section):**

> "Simple workflow: 1) Work on source machine, 2) Startup/awaken target machine if not started yet, 3) Manual trigger sync from source to target, 4) Wait for sync to complete, 5) Resume work"

From **Feature Breakdown**, the MVP consists of:
- **Items 1-4 (Core/Infrastructure — P1 priorities)**:
  1. Basic CLI & Infrastructure
  2. Safety Infrastructure (btrfs snapshot management)
  3. Installation & Setup
  4. Rollback Capability
  
- **Items 5-6 (User Features — the actual sync jobs)**:
  5. **User Data Sync** — `/home` and `/root` with metadata, selective caches, exclusions, conflict detection
  6. **Package Management Sync** — apt/snap/flatpak/PPAs/manual installs, detect conflicts/version mismatches

**Key user story constraint**: "Only one machine is actively used at a time" — no bi-directional sync in MVP.

---

## 2. Sync Home Directory: Technical Design

**Requirements (High Level Requirements, "What Must Sync"):**

> "User data (`/home`, `/root`)
> - All documents, code, configs, application data
> - Selective caches: dev tool caches (`uv`, `pip`, `cargo`, `npm`) synced; browser/IDE caches excluded
> - Specific mention for VS Code"

**Critical constraint (Environment section):**

> "All machines have a single btrfs filesystem with a flat layout (all synced data on one filesystem)"

**Technical approach NOT yet specified.** The detailed job spec for User Data Sync doesn't exist in the docs yet. The architecture allows for task-specific implementations (bash scripts via Python orchestration per ADR-003), but no decision on rsync vs. btrfs snapshots vs. custom sync method is documented.

---

## 3. Sync Packages: Technical Design

**Requirements (High Level Requirements):**

> "Installed packages: apt, snap, flatpak, manual .debs, custom PPAs, packages installed through install scripts"
> 
> "Each includes sync logic, conflict detection, and validation."

**Technical approach: NOT yet detailed.** No job implementation or spec exists. The Feature Breakdown says packages must "detect package conflicts or version mismatches," but the actual mechanism (list comparison, dpkg/rpm query, etc.) is unspecified.

---

## 4. Locked-In Architectural Constraints

**ADR-002 (SSH Communication Channel)**:
- Single persistent SSH connection with multiplexing (`ControlMaster`), not per-operation
- Target-side scripts produce **line-buffered stdout** for progress (no polling)
- Exit codes for success/failure; stdout/stderr separated
- "Forbidden": custom protocols, daemons on target, per-operation SSH connections

**ADR-003 (Python + Task-Specific Languages)**:
- Python orchestrator (Python 3.14, `uv` for dependency management, `asyncssh` library)
- Task-specific bash scripts for individual sync operations (bundled in `scripts/`)
- All orchestration logic in Python; delegation to bash where appropriate

**ADR-005 (Asyncio for Concurrency)**:
- **All Job methods MUST be `async def`** (`validate()`, `execute()`)
- Use `asyncssh` for remote operations (cancellable)
- Use `asyncio.TaskGroup` for structured concurrency
- Signal handlers trigger `asyncio.Task.cancel()` for immediate abort

**ADR-010 (Logging Infrastructure)**:
- stdlib `logging` module with custom Handler
- File logging (JSON), TUI logging (Rich-formatted)
- Configurable log levels separately for file and TUI

---

## 5. Job Abstraction

**From `/src/pcswitcher/jobs/base.py`:**

```python
class Job(ABC):
    name: ClassVar[str]
    required: ClassVar[bool]
    CONFIG_SCHEMA: ClassVar[dict] = {}
    
    # Async interface
    async def validate(self) -> list[ValidationError]: ...
    async def execute(self) -> None: ...
    
    # Helpers provided to jobs
    @property
    def source(self) -> LocalExecutor: ...  # For local operations
    @property
    def target(self) -> RemoteExecutor: ...  # For remote SSH operations
    
    def _log(host: Host, level: LogLevel, message: str, **extra) -> None
    def _report_progress(update: ProgressUpdate) -> None
    def _validation_error(host: Host, message: str) -> ValidationError
```

**Subclasses:**
- `SystemJob(Job)` — infrastructure (btrfs snapshots, installation); `required=True`, not in config
- `SyncJob(Job)` — user-facing features; `required=False`, configurable via `sync_jobs` in config.yaml
- `BackgroundJob(Job)` — parallel execution (disk monitoring); runs throughout sync

**Every sync job gets**:
- Configuration validation via JSON Schema (declared in `CONFIG_SCHEMA`)
- Logging at 6 levels (DEBUG, FULL, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL)
- Progress reporting with percent/current/total/item
- SSH executors for both source and target
- Graceful cancellation via `asyncio.CancelledError`

---

## Summary for MVP Sprint

To reach "sync home dir + sync packages fast":

1. ✅ **Infrastructure (already implemented)**: Core orchestrator, SSH layer, asyncio concurrency, btrfs snapshot jobs, logging
2. ❌ **Blocking issues (3 of them per Issue Triage 2025-12-31)**:
   - #103: Integrate stdlib logging (diagnostics for third-party libs)
   - #37: Add `--dry-run` flag (safety before sync)
   - #47: Warn on consecutive same-source syncs (prevent overwrite)
3. ⏳ **Then implement the two sync jobs** as `SyncJob` subclasses following the abstraction above

The technical *how* for each sync job (rsync? btrfs send? apt-mark? dpkg query?) is **not yet specified in the docs** — this is executor-level detail left to the job implementer to design.</result>

<result>Based on my comprehensive exploration, here's your critical assessment:

## Test Suite Summary

**Size**: 443 tests across 37 files
- 343 unit tests (4,849 lines)
- 40 contract tests (635 lines)  
- 60 integration tests (357 lines)
- Plus 2,300+ lines of infrastructure shell scripts

---

## What Makes Test Setup HEAVY

1. **VM Infrastructure Complexity** (the elephant in the room):
   - Two persistent Hetzner Cloud VMs (pc1, pc2) with btrfs filesystems
   - ~2,300 lines of shell scripts: `/home/janfr/dev/pc-switcher/tests/integration/scripts/`
     - VM provisioning via rescue mode + `installimage` (create-vm.sh: 338 lines)
     - VM configuration (configure-vm.sh: 141 lines)
     - Network setup, SSH keypairs, firewall (configure-hosts.sh)
     - Lock management via Hetzner server labels (not local files)
     - btrfs snapshot creation/rollback for reset
   - Reset flow (reset-vm.sh) requires: unmount subvolumes → mv old → snapshot new → reboot (~20-60s per VM)
   - Lock acquisition/release prevents concurrent runs (necessary but adds latency)

2. **Fixture Heavy Integration Tests** (conftest.py: 473 lines):
   - Module-scoped SSH connections to both VMs
   - Parallel executors (BashLoginRemoteExecutor wrapping SSH)
   - Installation fixtures (uv, GitHub releases, version management)
   - State cleanup fixtures (config removal, snapshot deletion)
   - 4 different installation scenarios (with/without pc-switcher, old versions)

3. **Test Environment Requirements**:
   - Integration tests require: HCLOUD_TOKEN, PC_SWITCHER_TEST_PC1_HOST, PC_SWITCHER_TEST_PC2_HOST, PC_SWITCHER_TEST_USER
   - Manual VM IP lookup + known_hosts updates (run-integration-tests.sh)
   - Credentials in GitHub secrets + env vars
   - Can't run locally without Hetzner account + API token

---

## Obvious Over-Engineering/Duplication

**Critical structural duplication**:
- `tests/unit_jobs/` (1 file: test_disk_space_monitor.py) — OLD location
- `tests/unit/jobs/` (3 files: test_dummy_jobs.py, test_install_on_target_job.py, test_snapshot_job.py) — NEW location
- `tests/unit/test_jobs/` (empty directory, orphaned)
- `tests/integration/jobs/` (1 file for integration-specific job test)

**Migration incomplete**: `unit_jobs/` should be deleted; `unit/test_jobs/` doesn't exist; inconsistent naming across 4 job-related directories.

**Contract Tests: Marginal ROI**:
- 40 contract tests, 635 lines, but testing mostly trivial interface compliance
- test_job_interface.py: Verifies jobs have `name`, `CONFIG_SCHEMA`, `validate()`, `execute()` — essentially interface type-checks
- test_executor_contract.py: Verifies mock executor has same methods as real one (14 trivial tests like "run_command returns CommandResult")
- test_logging_contract.py: Checks JSON/Rich formatters produce valid output (15 tests)
- **Value**: Low. These should either be unit tests of the actual implementation OR removed if mocks are trusted. Separate "contract" layer adds ceremony without catching real bugs.

**Integration Test Benchmarks** (tests/integration/benchmarks/):
- Excluded from CI by default (`-m "integration and not benchmark"`)
- Seems to measure SSH overhead (~80ms per call)
- Questionable: benchmarking infrastructure overhead isn't actionable without context (what's acceptable?)

---

## Unit Tests: Fast & Standalone?

**Yes, they run fast** (~30 seconds for full suite, per docs):
```bash
uv run pytest tests/unit tests/contract -v
```
No external infra needed. Mocks are extensive (mock_executor, mock_connection, mock_event_bus in conftest.py).

**BUT**: 343 tests in 4,849 lines = ~14 lines per test (heavy fixtures). Many tests have setup boilerplate; lots of mocking. Tests are thorough but not lean.

---

## Contract Test Layer: Is It Pulling Weight?

**No. Kill it (or merge into unit tests).**

Rationale:
- Tests trivial interface compliance ("does this method exist?" "does it return the right type?")
- Mock/real executor parity isn't the risk — implementation bugs are the risk
- More value in unit tests that exercise behavior (success/failure paths, edge cases)
- Adds 635 lines of ceremony without catching real logic errors
- If you don't trust mocks, mocks aren't the solution anyway

---

## Infrastructure Test Requirements

**Integration tests need**:
- **VMs**: Two CX23 instances (~EUR 7/month total)
- **Provisioning**: btrfs root filesystem (not standard Linux — required Hetzner rescue mode + installimage)
- **SSH**: Key-based auth, known_hosts management
- **Locking**: Hetzner API access to set server labels (prevents race conditions)
- **Reset**: Btrfs snapshot rollback (fast) rather than VM recreation (slower)
- **State isolation**: Tests must clean up after themselves (VM state shared within session, reset only between sessions)

**Not needed** (good news):
- Load balancers, databases, or complex infra
- Docker/containers (bare metal VMs)
- File storage beyond default filesystem

---

## Blunt Assessment: Over-Engineered & What's Essential

### ❌ Over-Engineered (Delete or Simplify)
| What | Why | Action |
|------|-----|--------|
| `tests/unit_jobs/` | Incomplete migration, now redundant with `unit/jobs/` | Delete directory |
| `tests/unit/test_jobs/` | Empty; orphaned directory | Delete directory |
| **Contract tests** (635 lines) | Trivial interface checks; low signal | Merge useful tests into unit suite or delete entirely |
| **Benchmarks** (test_executor_overhead.py) | Measures SSH overhead; excludes from CI; unclear actionability | Delete unless you need this data regularly |
| Lock/retry logic in run-integration-tests.sh | 2300+ LOC of shell for VM orchestration — necessary but verbose. Could move to Python. | As-is for now; consider Python rewrite if touched again |

### ✅ Essential (Keep)
| What | Why | Keep |
|------|-----|------|
| **Unit tests** (343 tests, ~4900 LOC) | Fast, safe, catch logic errors locally | Yes, but prune unnecessary fixtures |
| **Integration tests** (60 tests) | Real btrfs, SSH, destructive ops — can't mock safely | Yes; vm-isolated testing is non-negotiable |
| **VM infrastructure** (provisioning, reset) | VM isolation is core safety model | Yes; but document better, consider Python rewrite |
| **Hetzner lock mechanism** | Prevents concurrent test runs corrupting shared VMs | Yes |
| **btrfs snapshot reset** | Fast reset without recreating VMs | Yes |

---

## Recommended Quick Wins

1. **Delete orphaned directories**: `unit_jobs/`, `unit/test_jobs/` (5 min)
2. **Consolidate job tests**: Move `tests/unit_jobs/test_disk_space_monitor.py` → `tests/unit/jobs/` (2 min)
3. **Decide on contract tests**: Either delete all 40 tests or merge important ones into unit suite (30 min decision + execution)
4. **Document infrastructure assumptions**: Add comment block in `/home/janfr/dev/pc-switcher/tests/run-integration-tests.sh` explaining why 2300 LOC is unavoidable (why not hcloud CLI, why not cloud-init, why btrfs snapshots not Hetzner snapshots, etc.)

## Core Problem (Not Quick-Fixable)

The **real complexity isn't bugs; it's the problem domain**. PC-switcher does destructive filesystem ops as root. Safe testing requires:
- VM isolation (can't test locally)
- btrfs snapshots (kernel feature, not a library)
- SSH orchestration (two-machine sync)
- Concurrency control (shared VMs)

The framework reflects the problem, not poor design choices. **The over-engineering is organizational (duplicate directories), not architectural.**

---

**File references**:
- `/home/janfr/dev/pc-switcher/tests/unit_jobs/` — DELETE
- `/home/janfr/dev/pc-switcher/tests/unit/test_jobs/` — DELETE
- `/home/janfr/dev/pc-switcher/tests/contract/` — CONSIDER DELETING (635 lines, 40 tests of low value)
- `/home/janfr/dev/pc-switcher/tests/integration/scripts/` — KEEP (2300 LOC, necessary)
- `/home/janfr/dev/pc-switcher/tests/conftest.py` — REVIEW for bloat (106 lines, mostly OK)
- `/home/janfr/dev/pc-switcher/tests/integration/conftest.py` — NECESSARY (473 lines, fixture-heavy but unavoidable)</result>

</task-notification>

lastPrompt: I haven't been working on this project for months. I remember we were stuck on tests and I felt completely overwhelmed by my efforts to get tests to be well structured, complete, efficient and useful.…

customTitle: evaluate pc-switcher status + way of working

aiTitle: Assess project status and streamline path to first useful features

agentName: evaluate pc-switcher status + way of working

content.text:
I now have the full picture, cross-checked against the running code, the tests, the requirements, and the issue tracker. Here's the honest assessment.

## Status report

**What you actually have (and it's solid):**
The entire *machine* around sync is built, working, and green — 432 unit/contract tests pass in 2.7s. Concretely, `pc-switcher sync <target>` runs a real 10-phase pipeline: source lock → SSH (asyncssh, multiplexed) → target lock → job discovery/validation → disk preflight → **pre-sync btrfs snapshots** → install/upgrade pc-switcher on target → config sync (with diff + prompt) → execute jobs → **post-sync snapshots** → history tracking. Plus CLI (`sync/init/logs/cleanup-snapshots/self update`), `--dry-run`, consecutive-sync guard, Rich TUI, structured logging, and a clean `Job` abstraction (`SyncJob`/`SystemJob`/`BackgroundJob`). The would-be "blockers" from the Dec triage (dry-run, logging, consecutive-sync) are **already done**.

**What you don't have — the entire point of the product:**

| Feature | Issue | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Home sync (`/home`, `/root`) | #117 | OPEN, never started |
| Package sync (apt/snap/flatpak) | #118 | OPEN, never started |
| System config sync | #119 | OPEN, never started |

There are exactly **zero** real `SyncJob`s. The only sync jobs that exist are `dummy_success`/`dummy_fail`. In `default-config.yaml`, `user_data` and `packages` are commented out as "future jobs."

**The diagnosis, bluntly:** ~660 commits, Nov–Jan, then dead for 5 months. Effort went almost entirely into (a) the harness and (b) the *test infrastructure for the harness*. Of ~30 open issues, roughly half are `comp:testing-framework`. You have **12.3k lines of test code guarding 6.1k lines of source** — a 2:1 ratio — and that source is all plumbing. You built a beautifully tested engine and never attached the wheels. A real job is ~130 lines (`install_on_target.py` is your template).

## Why the test work felt bottomless

The test agent confirmed it's partly organizational sprawl, partly genuine domain difficulty:

- **Sprawl (cheap to fix):** four overlapping job-test directories (`unit_jobs/`, `unit/jobs/`, an empty `unit/test_jobs/`, `integration/jobs/`); a 635-line **contract-test layer** that mostly asserts "does this method exist / return the right type" — low signal; a benchmarks dir excluded from CI.
- **Genuine (not fixable):** integration tests do destructive root-level btrfs/SSH ops, so they need two real btrfs VMs (Hetzner), provisioning scripts, snapshot-reset, and a lock. That ~2,300 lines of shell is *inherent to the problem*, not bad design. The trap was treating it as something to perfect rather than something to use minimally.

The real failure mode: **chasing test completeness/elegance on infrastructure instead of shipping a vertical slice and testing that.**

## Proposed way forward

The throughline: **stop investing in the harness; spend the next sessions only on vertical product slices, tested proportionately.**

### 1. Freeze the framework backlog
Put every `comp:testing-framework` and harness-refactor issue (#65, #69, #76, #68, #62, #82, #126, #30, #28, etc.) into a `status: parked` bucket. Do **not** touch them until a real job needs them. The only test cleanup worth doing now is ~15 minutes: delete `unit_jobs/` and empty `unit/test_jobs/`, and decide the contract layer is frozen (don't grow it).

### 2. Ship Home Sync as the first vertical slice (#117)
This is your "first useful functionality." It's a single `SyncJob` subclass. The one **real decision** is the transport, and it's worth deciding deliberately because your requirements ("sync dev caches, exclude browser/IDE caches") force the answer:

- **rsync over SSH** (`rsync -aAX --delete --exclude-from=...`): file-level, supports selective excludes, battle-tested, preserves ACLs/xattrs. Tension with ADR-002 (it opens its own ssh channel, not your asyncssh connection) — but btrfs snapshots already give you the rollback safety, so this is pragmatic and I'd recommend it.
- **btrfs send/receive** of `@home`: atomic and exact, fits "near-complete replication" — but it replaces the whole subvolume and **can't do per-path excludes**, which your cache rules require. So it's disqualified for home unless you drop selective excludes.

My recommendation: **rsync for home**, accept the ADR-002 nuance, write a one-line ADR amendment noting why.

### 3. Then Package Sync (#118)
More mechanical, no transport question: on both machines capture `apt-mark showmanual`, `snap list`, `flatpak list --app --columns=application`; diff source-minus-target; install the delta on target. Conflict/version handling can be v2 — **v1 = "make target's package set a superset of source."**

### 4. Testing, proportionate (this is the behavior change)
For each job: unit-test the *logic* (exclude-rule building, package-set diffing) with the existing mock executors — that's where bugs live and it's fast. Then **exactly one** happy-path integration test per job on the VMs you already have. No new contract tests, no benchmark tests, no new fixtures unless the job genuinely needs one. "Good quality" here means the diff logic is correct and one real end-to-end run works — not exhaustive matrix coverage of plumbing.

### 5. Process: GSD lightweight, keep ADRs, drop SpecKit
Your instinct is right. The three features are tagged `ai:needs-speckit`, but full SpecKit specs are exactly the overhead that sank you. Use GSD `/gsd-mvp-phase` (vertical-slice framing) per job. Keep ADRs for genuine architecture forks (like the rsync-vs-btrfs decision above). I would **not** write a multi-document spec for a 130-line job.

## Where I want to spar

Two decisions are genuinely yours, and they change what I'd plan:

1. **Home-sync transport** — do you accept **rsync** (selective excludes, slight ADR-002 deviation) as I recommend, or do you want to preserve the pure-asyncssh / btrfs-send model and drop selective cache excludes for v1?
2. **Scope of "first useful"** — is the goal *home sync alone* shipped and dogfooded first (fastest dopamine, ~one focused session), or do you want home + packages together before calling it useful?

If you point me at #1 and #2, I'll spin up a GSD MVP phase for the first slice and we start writing the actual product. Want me to also park the framework issues on GitHub now as the first concrete step?

content: Goal: get pc-switcher to its first useful feature fast. The framework is fully built and tested, but no real sync jobs exist yet. Next: you decide home-sync transport (rsync vs btrfs) and whether to ship home alone or with packages, then I start the first job. (disable recaps in /config)

input.description: Analyze home-sync transport options
input.prompt:
You are analyzing a technical transport decision for "PC-switcher" (a tool that syncs a Linux home directory from a source machine to a target machine over SSH; both run Ubuntu 24.04 on a single btrfs filesystem). I need a rigorous, evidence-based comparison of two ways to sync the home directory. Do NOT write code. Produce a decision memo.

STEP 1 — Establish the constraints from the actual repo (quote exact text, cite file paths):
- Read docs/adr/adr-002-ssh-communication-channel.md IN FULL. I need to know precisely what it forbids/mandates. Critical question: does ADR-002 forbid using the `rsync` CLI (which opens its OWN ssh channel) — or does it only govern how the Python orchestrator's control/communication channel works (i.e. asyncssh for orchestration, but data-transfer tools are out of scope)? Quote the "Forbidden"/"Decision"/"Consequences" sections verbatim.
- Read docs/adr/adr-003-implementation-language.md — does it explicitly allow delegating to task-specific tools/bash (e.g. rsync, btrfs) for individual sync operations, or mandate pure-Python/asyncssh for data movement? Quote.
- Read src/pcswitcher/btrfs_snapshots.py and src/pcswitcher/jobs/btrfs.py to determine: how does the harness already create snapshots (read-only? subvol layout — the config uses "@" and "@home")? Are pre-sync snapshots available on BOTH source and target at job-execution time? This matters because option B reuses them.

STEP 2 — Compare these two transports for syncing /home:
  OPTION A — rsync over SSH: `rsync -aAX --delete --exclude-from=<rules>` source→target. File-level, supports per-path excludes (sync dev caches like uv/pip/cargo/npm; exclude browser/IDE caches).
  OPTION B — btrfs send/receive WITH exception handling: take a new read-only snapshot of source @home, `btrfs send` it (incrementally, with `-p` parent where possible) to target, `btrfs receive`; then on the target, overwrite the "excepted" paths (the caches/folders that must NOT be replaced) with their versions from the pre-sync snapshot the harness already took on the target right before. Net effect: full-fidelity replication of home EXCEPT the excepted paths are restored to target's own prior state.

Compare across AT LEAST these criteria, and add any I'm missing:
- Speed: first/full sync vs incremental subsequent syncs. (btrfs send -p sends only changed extents; rsync must stat/checksum the tree.)
- Correctness/fidelity: ownership, ACLs, xattrs, hardlinks, sparse files, special files, exact replication vs file-level. Crash consistency / atomicity if interrupted mid-sync.
- SSD wear: bytes actually written to the target SSD in each approach (CoW behavior, whether unchanged blocks are rewritten, whether the "restore excepted paths" step in B causes extra writes, whether rsync rewrites whole files vs blocks).
- Selective-exclude capability and how awkward the excepted-path mechanism is in each.
- Resumability / interruptibility (asyncio cancellation mid-sync), and integration with the existing pre/post btrfs snapshot rollback safety net.
- Space overhead on target during the operation (B's receive creates a new subvol = transient 2x home?).
- Operational: dry-run/preview support, debuggability, complexity of implementation, coupling to btrfs-on-both-ends.
- Conflict/divergence detection.

STEP 3 — Give a clear recommendation with rationale, and note under what conditions the other option wins. Be concrete and quantitative where you can. Flag any place where ADR-002/003 would need an explicit amendment.

Return a tight, well-structured memo (markdown headers, a comparison table, then recommendation). This is going to a technical owner who will challenge weak claims — label anything you infer vs. verify.

input.description: Audit open issues for MVP blockers
input.prompt:
You are auditing the open GitHub issues of the "PC-switcher" repo to find anything that would BLOCK or must be fixed before implementing the MVP: a new `SyncJob` subclass that syncs the user's home directory from source to target machine, then running it via the existing `pc-switcher sync <target>` pipeline for real.

Context: the sync pipeline already works end-to-end with dummy jobs (lock → SSH via asyncssh → target lock → validate → pre-snapshot → install pc-switcher on target → config sync → execute jobs → post-snapshot → history). The MVP just adds a real home-sync job into that existing machinery. We are NOT touching the test framework now.

Do this:
1. Run `gh issue list --state open --limit 100` and get the full list.
2. For each issue that plausibly touches: the core sync pipeline (comp:core), the Job/orchestrator framework, SSH/asyncssh communication, install-on-target, locking, snapshots, or running a real destructive sync — read its body with `gh issue view <n>`. Skip issues that are purely about the TEST framework (comp:testing-framework) UNLESS they describe a real product bug that would also bite a real sync (e.g. a lock not being released would affect real runs too).
3. Pay particular attention to these known ones and judge their MVP impact: #79 (GitHub API rate limits when running InstallOnTargetJob on target), #82 (remove GitHub API dependency from core), #78 (lock not cleared when run cancelled), #126 (unify source/target execution / duplicate bash commands), #30 (remove 1-job-module=1-Job-class constraint — does this block ADDING a new job?), #28 (DAG for jobs), #62 (missing real integration tests — P0), #87 (ssh host key checking), #48 (CLI flags for interactive prompts).

Classify EVERY issue you examined into exactly one bucket:
- 🔴 BLOCKER — must fix before the home-sync job can be built or run correctly/safely.
- 🟠 URGENT-BUG — a real correctness/safety bug that will bite a real sync but has a workaround; fix soon, not strictly blocking.
- 🟡 PARK — safe to defer; doesn't affect MVP.
- ⚪ IRRELEVANT/test-only.

For each 🔴 and 🟠, give: issue number, one-line what, and one-line WHY it does/doesn't block, citing the issue body. Be skeptical and concrete — do not inflate severity. Most issues are probably PARK. The deliverable is a short triage table plus a 2-3 sentence bottom line: "Before building home-sync, you must fix: X, Y (or: nothing — proceed)."

<result>## Triage: Open issues vs. home-sync MVP

I listed all 31 open issues and read every core/pipeline-relevant one in detail (79, 82, 78, 126, 30, 28, 62, 87, 48, 117, 118, 119, 31, 29, 24, 23, 26). I verified two load-bearing claims against the source.

### Verified facts
- `InstallOnTargetJob` does hit the GitHub API on a real sync (`install_on_target.py:106` → `get_release_floor()` → `version.py:get_releases()`), but the code already "minimize[s] github lookups" (`install_on_target.py:97`). One real sync makes ~1-2 calls — well under the 60/hr unauthenticated limit. The rate-limit pain is test-volume only.
- The "1 module = 1 Job class" constraint (#30): adding a single new `HomeSyncJob` in its own module is exactly the supported case. Existing jobs (`dummy_success.py`, `dummy_fail.py`, `btrfs.py`, `install_on_target.py`) are already one-class-per-module.

### Result: no 🔴 BLOCKERS, no 🟠 URGENT-BUGs for the MVP path

| # | What | Bucket | Why |
|---|------|--------|-----|
| 117 | Feature 5: Home Sync | n/a | This *is* the MVP target, not a blocker. |
| 79 | GITHUB_TOKEN not passed → rate limit | 🟡 PARK | Mechanism is real, but bites only at test volume; one real sync stays under 60/hr. Latest comment: "scope of this ticket is only testing." |
| 82 | Remove GitHub API dep from core | 🟡 PARK | Cleanup refactor; a single real sync's couple of API calls are harmless. |
| 126 | Unify source/target execution | 🟡 PARK | Current dual Python/bash impl works end-to-end; pure maintenance refactor. |
| 30 | Remove 1-module=1-class constraint | 🟡 PARK | Does NOT block adding a job — one new module/class fits the existing rule. Only matters if you want 2 classes in 1 file. |
| 28 | DAG for Jobs | 🟡 PARK | Jobs already run in a defined sequence; home-sync is just one more job in that sequence. |
| 62 | Missing real integration tests (P0) | 🟡 PARK (test) | Real concern that home-sync's real behavior would be unverified, but it's test coverage — and you said the test framework is explicitly out of scope now. |
| 48 | CLI flags for interactive prompts | 🟡 PARK | Only bites non-interactive/automated runs. A human running `pc-switcher sync` interactively can answer prompts. |
| 31 | Rollback CLI command | 🟡 PARK | Pipeline already takes a pre-snapshot on the target before jobs run; rollback CLI is convenience, not a safety prerequisite. btrfs rollback can be done manually if needed. |
| 78 | Lock not cleared on cancelled CI run | ⚪ test-only | Body is specifically about the integration-tests.yml workflow lock fixture being killed by GitHub's `cancel`, not the orchestrator's machine lock. See note below. |
| 87 | Stricter SSH host checking in run-integration-tests.sh | ⚪ test-only | Lives in test harness script, not product. |
| 118, 119 | Package / System-config sync | 🟡 PARK | Different features, not home-sync. |
| 29, 24, 23, 26 | Forever-jobs, optional DiskSpaceMonitor, drop-btrfs, drop-Ubuntu-24.04 | 🟡 PARK | MVP targets the existing btrfs/Ubuntu-24.04 happy path; none are on the home-sync path. |
| 156,153,150,132,88,85,76,69,68,65,64,46,40 | (test-framework, docs, VM mgmt, VSCode-sync) | ⚪ | Not on the core sync path. |

### One thing worth a 5-minute check (not an issue, no ticket)
#78 is about the *CI workflow* lock, but it raises an adjacent real-sync question: does the orchestrator release the *machine* lock if a real destructive home-sync is `Ctrl-C`'d mid-run? That's product behavior, not covered by #78. If it isn't already handled, a stale lock would block re-running — a nuisance, not data-loss (snapshots protect data). Worth confirming the orchestrator's lock cleanup is in a `finally`/signal handler before running destructive syncs, but it does not block *building* the job.

### Bottom line
Before building home-sync: nothing — proceed. None of the open issues block adding a `HomeSyncJob` to the existing pipeline or running it for real. The frequently-cited ones (#79/#82 GitHub API, #30 job constraint, #28 DAG, #126 unification, #62 tests) are either test-volume-only, refactors, or explicitly out of scope; the destructive-sync safety net (target pre-snapshot) already exists in the pipeline. Just confirm the orchestrator releases its machine lock on interrupt before your first real destructive run.</result>

### One thing worth a 5-minute check (not an issue, no ticket)
#78 is about the *CI workflow* lock, but it raises an adjacent real-sync question: does the orchestrator release the *machine* lock if a real destructive home-sync is `Ctrl-C`'d mid-run? That's product behavior, not covered by #78. If it isn't already handled, a stale lock would block re-running — a nuisance, not data-loss (snapshots protect data). Worth confirming the orchestrator's lock cleanup is in a `finally`/signal handler before running destructive syncs, but it does not block *building* the job.

### Bottom line
Before building home-sync: nothing — proceed. None of the open issues block adding a `HomeSyncJob` to the existing pipeline or running it for real. The frequently-cited ones (#79/#82 GitHub API, #30 job constraint, #28 DAG, #126 unification, #62 tests) are either test-volume-only, refactors, or explicitly out of scope; the destructive-sync safety net (target pre-snapshot) already exists in the pipeline. Just confirm the orchestrator releases its machine lock on interrupt before your first real destructive run.</result>
